# ASTRA Project Memory Export

**Project:** ASTRA (Autonomous Security Testing and Reconnaissance Agent) — also referred to as "PwnPilot" in the formal proposal document
**Date:** June 20, 2026
**Prepared by:** Wassam
**Generated by:** Claude (Anthropic), via project chat session
**Scope note:** This document is extracted from project context only (uploaded files + chat history). It is not authoritative — verify against the actual repo and latest team discussions before relying on it.

---

## 1. Project Identity

- **Title:** ASTRA — Autonomous Security Testing and Reconnaissance Agent
  (Proposal document title: "PwnPilot: Autonomous Penetration Testing RAG-based Multi-Agent")
- **Type:** Final Year Project (FYP), BSSE, MAJU
- **Supervisor:** Hafiz Muhammad Attaullah
- **Team:**
  - **Wassam (Syed Wassam Haider)** — Builds the agent orchestration (LangGraph), the Scanner and Exploit agents, and leads the system architecture presentation/demo
  - **Maaz (Muhammad Maaz Mashhood)** — Builds the knowledge-retrieval pipeline (RAG), the Recon agent, and leads the problem statement/novelty presentation
  - **Arham (Muhammad Arham Rashid)** — Builds the accuracy-scoring model for results, designs the experiments, generates the PDF report, and leads the results/conclusions presentation

---

## 2. Research Core (Plain Language)

**The big idea:** Build an AI system that can automatically test a website or server for security weaknesses — the way a human security expert would — using only free, locally-run AI models instead of expensive paid ones like ChatGPT.

**The problem with existing tools:**
- They rely on paid AI (like GPT-4), which costs money and risks privacy (your data goes to an outside company).
- They tend to "make things up" — inventing fake vulnerabilities or fake fixes that sound real but aren't.
- Most don't compare different AI models against each other in a fair, structured way.

**ASTRA's answer:**
- Use three free, locally-run AI models instead of paid ones.
- Give the AI a "lookup library" of real, verified security knowledge (known vulnerabilities, attack patterns) so it stops guessing and starts checking facts — this is the RAG part.
- Split the work across four specialized AI helpers instead of one AI doing everything: one explores the target, one scans for weaknesses, one tries safe exploitation, one writes the final report.

**The experiment (the academic core):**
- Test 3 different free AI models, each either *with* or *without* the fact-checking library — that's 3 models × 2 conditions = 6 setups, run 3 times each = 18 total test runs.
- All tests run against a deliberately vulnerable practice website (DVWA) built for this kind of testing.
- The goal: prove whether giving the AI a "fact-checking library" actually makes it more accurate and less likely to invent false results.

**What makes this new (novelty claim):**
- No prior published system combines free/local AI models *with* a fact-checking library for this kind of security testing — past systems used either paid AI with no fact-checking, or fact-checking with paid AI, never the combination ASTRA uses.
- One major prior project (PentestGPT) openly said this combination was something they hadn't gotten to yet — ASTRA closes that gap.
- Another prior project (HackingBuddyGPT) only tested 12 cases; ASTRA's 18 test runs is a stronger, more defensible sample size, and is comparable to other accepted academic work using small benchmarks.

---

## 3. Architecture Snapshot (as of today)

**Built / working:**
- Four AI helpers (Recon, Scanner, Exploit, Report) coordinated together
- The "fact-checking library" (RAG) is built — it pulls from a database of known vulnerabilities (CVE/NVD), a catalog of attack techniques (MITRE ATT&CK, 600+ entries), and an exploit-code reference list (ExploitDB)
- Three free AI models running locally (Llama 3.1, Mistral 7B, DeepSeek-R1), tuned to run efficiently on a single home-lab graphics card
- Security testing tools wired in (Nmap, SQLMap, Hydra, Nikto, Gobuster); one tool (Searchsploit) excluded due to a technical Windows-only incompatibility
- A simple web dashboard (built with Streamlit) to search vulnerabilities, search attack techniques, and chat with the AI
- Target practice environment (DVWA) is set up and running

**In progress:**
- Finalizing the literature review (aiming for 10–15 strongest papers, selected from a wider pool of ~27 gathered)
- Producing the final results — charts comparing performance with vs. without the fact-checking library
- Arham's method for measuring how often the AI "makes things up" (hallucination rate), using real vulnerability records as the answer key
- Diagrams required for the academic write-up (flow charts, trend charts, comparison tables, etc.)

**Not started / not yet confirmed:**
- Final compiled results report combining all 18 test runs
- Final defense materials / talking points for the academic panel

---

## 4. Key Decisions Log

- **Use only free, locally-run AI models (no paid AI)** — keeps the project private, cost-free, and avoids reliance on outside companies
- **Add a fact-checking library (RAG) grounded in real vulnerability/attack data** — directly reduces the AI "making things up," which is the main risk in security tools
- **Split work across four specialized AI helpers instead of one** — matches how real security testing is divided into phases, and prior research shows team-based AI outperforms a single AI
- **Use 18 total test runs (3 AI models × with/without fact-checking × 3 repeats)** — gives a statistically defensible, comparable sample size against prior academic work
- **Frame the AI as "choosing from a list of known fixes" rather than "writing new attack code from scratch"** — smaller AI models aren't reliable at writing original exploit code, so this avoids a major source of errors
- **Switch exploit-reference data source to a GitLab-hosted list instead of the original website** — original source was hard to read automatically (it loaded content dynamically)
- **Clean up scan results before feeding them to the fact-checking library** — raw scan output was confusing the system; cleaned, simplified input gives much better results
- **Exclude Burp Suite from the toolset** — it requires a paid license, which conflicts with the project's "fully free and open" positioning
- **Cap the AI's "memory" at a fixed size during long tasks** — prevents one of the three AI models from running out of working memory mid-task

---

## 5. Open Problems

- Finalizing which ~10–15 papers make the final literature review cut (from the larger pool of 27)
- Completing the final results charts and comparison data for the paper
- Confirming and validating Arham's method for scoring how often the AI invents false results
- Preparing clear, confident answers for tough questions the academic panel might ask
- Producing all required supporting diagrams for the academic submission (paper selection flow chart, trend charts, comparison tables, gap analysis visuals, timeline)

---

## 6. Source Inventory

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `ASTRA_Literature_Review.docx` | Full write-up of 27 research papers related to ASTRA, grouped into 7 topic areas, each with strengths, weaknesses, and how ASTRA improves on it |
| `ASTRA_Section10_GapTable.docx` | A detailed comparison table scoring 20 of those papers against ASTRA across 6 key capabilities, plus the formal 5-point novelty argument |
| `ASTRA_Statistical_Analysis.docx` | Statistics on the global cybersecurity crisis (attack growth, vulnerability counts, workforce shortage, market size) used to justify why ASTRA is needed |
| `FYP Project Proposal template 1.docx` | The official FYP proposal form — team details, problem statement, objectives, features, methodology, and references |

---

## 7. Next Steps (Priority Order)

1. Finalize the literature review paper selection (narrow 27 down to 10–15)
2. Complete the final benchmarking results and comparison charts (with vs. without fact-checking library)
3. Finish and validate Arham's accuracy/hallucination scoring method
4. Produce the remaining required diagrams for the written report
5. Prepare and rehearse academic defense talking points, especially around novelty and benchmark size
